description:
Our PINCH Multifamily Sales Training Program is designed to empower cleaning professionals with the knowledge and skills required to sell their cleaning services to multifamily properties, while using PINCH as their support system.

Led by an expert trainer with extensive experience in the field, this program covers key topics, including understanding the multifamily market, mastering PINCH's service portfolio, effective sales techniques, and ethical considerations.

This training module will give you practical insights through real-life simulations, and access to valuable sales resources.

Whether you're a seasoned cleaning company owner who has sold their services over and over, or completely new to the industry, this training will provide you with the tools and strategies needed to thrive in the competitive multifamily space and achieve sales success.

Expanding your business to the multifamily industry is not only easy, but life changing when done correctly.

Follow us as we guide you through to business success.

video.subtitle: Welcome and Program Overview
video.description:
Welcome to the PINCH Sales Training Program! Today, we're diving into how our unique approach has opened doors for small to midsize cleaning teams like yours into the lucrative multifamily market. Get ready to transform your business!

Did you know each multifamily building you contract can add over $1,250 to your monthly revenue? That's right! In this training, we'll show you the strategies to not only meet but exceed the strict requirements of this industry, ensuring you clinch those high-value contracts.

So buckle up! By the end of our program, you'll be equipped with all the tools you need to effectively navigate and dominate the multifamily cleaning market. Let's make your business boom and turn opportunities into outstanding successes.

video.subtitle: Objectives
video.description:
Hello everyone, and welcome to our sales training program! Today, we start our journey to mastering the multifamily market. We'll begin by diving into the world of PINCH Services, exploring how our unique offerings can transform your cleaning business.

Next up, we're going to focus on honing your sales techniques, which is more about telling your life story as a cleaner than it is selling. It's all about making genuine connections with Property Managers, understanding their needs, and showing them why your services stand out.

Moving on, we'll tackle the exciting part: selling PINCH and your company's Services effectively. You'll learn the ins and outs of navigating objections, closing deals confidently, and managing your sales pipeline like a champion.

Throughout this program, our goal is not just to equip you with knowledge and skills but also to boost your confidence. By the end, you'll be ready to independently attract and secure clients in the multifamily space.

So, let's gear up and set your cleaning business on the path to unprecedented success in the multifamily industry. Together, we'll unlock new opportunities and reap the financial rewards. Let the adventure begin!

video.subtitle: Multifamily Market Overview
video.description:
In this video, we're diving into the bustling world of the multifamily industry, which includes all those apartment complexes and condos you see popping up everywhere. Thanks to shifts in housing trends and city living, there's a huge demand for your skilled services.

Now, let's chat about what makes this market really tick—tenant turnovers.

Imagine this: someone moves out, and soon after, someone new moves in. Property managers need these places painted, carpets cleaned and then the final deep cleaning, making the space look spotless as fast as possible.

Turnovers aren't just about sweeping and mopping; they're a golden opportunity. Every clean unit increases tenant happiness, boosts lease renewals, and elevates the property's reputation. It's your chance to and show just how crucial a spotless space is.

With the industry booming, more buildings mean more homes that need your magic touch...more frequently. It's not just about cleaning; it's about transforming spaces to welcome new beginnings. That's a big responsibility, right?

So, team, as we gear up to tackle these turnovers, remember, your role is key in turning an empty apartment into a welcoming home. Let's make those properties sparkle and keep the multifamily market thriving with your expert touch!

video.subtitle: Target Audience Analysis
video.description:
Understanding Key Players: Property Managers and Maintenance Supervisors (Service Managers/Maintenance Managers)

Property Managers - the primary decision-makers in multifamily properties. They are responsible for overseeing the day-to-day operations of apartment complexes and condominiums.

Their main objective is to ensure that properties run smoothly, tenants are satisfied, and the property maintains a positive reputation. Maintenance Supervisors - responsible for ensuring the overall upkeep and functionality of the community.

Their primary roles include overseeing maintenance teams, scheduling repairs, and ensuring that all units and common areas are well-maintained. Their goals and objectives revolve around timely and cost-effective property maintenance, tenant satisfaction, and adherence to safety regulations.

They play a vital role in maintaining the property's physical condition and addressing residents' maintenance requests promptly.

Frustrations: Property and Maintenance Supervisors face several challenges in their role, and many of their frustrations are related to cleaning services during turnovers:

Poor Cleaning Quality: Property managers often struggle with cleaning companies that fail to meet their high standards of cleanliness. This can lead to delays in getting a new tenant into a unit, resulting in lost revenue and tenant dissatisfaction.

Recurring Requests for Re-Cleaning: Due to subpar cleaning quality, Property Managers frequently need to request cleaning companies to return to address missed spots or unresolved issues,causing delays and tenant inconveniences.

Time-Consuming Management: Managing cleaning schedules, cleaner "no-shows", quality checks, and communication with cleaning companies can be a time-consuming task for Property Managers. It diverts their attention from other essential property management duties.

Accessibility of Property Managers and Service Managers: Property Managers are typically not easily accessible via email or phone
due to their busy schedules. However, they can often be reached through site visits and drop-ins to communities.

Establishing a personal connection and demonstrating commitment to meeting their specific needs can be key to gaining their trust and securing contracts. Maintenance Supervisors can typically be found around the community.

Many of these properties provide the Maintenance Supervisor with a golf cart or company vehicle to be more efficient at moving around the community. If you start to look for these vehicles in your sales efforts, you will find that many Service/Maintenance Managers are a wealth of information.

They will commonly tell you if they conduct turnover cleanings in-house or if they outsource to a cleaning company. They also tend to be honest about their opinions of the quality of those teams. Use this to your advantage!

video.subtitle: Competitive Landscape
video.description:
In the multifamily cleaning space, the competitive landscape has long been dominated by commercial cleaning companies that rely on a revolving door of hourly workers. However, this industry paradigm is shifting, and it presents an incredible opportunity for cleaning teams partnering with PINCH.

Here's why you're in an enviable position with the potential for remarkable success:

1. Quality and Consistency: Unlike traditional commercial cleaning companies that grapple with staggering turnover rates of 200 to 400 percent per year, you stand out as a beacon of stability and quality. Your team comprises dedicated professionals committed to their craft, resulting in unparalleled consistency and reliability.

2. Property Manager Frustrations: Property managers have long been frustrated by the inconsistency and subpar quality of cleaning services provided by hourly workers in the multifamily space. They're tired of dealing with the hassles of constantly changing cleaning crews and the subsequent drop in cleaning quality.

3. PINCH, The Game Changer: Enter PINCH, the game changer. You have the opportunity to offer property managers a breath of fresh air. You are the answer to their frustrations. Your company brings to the table not just high-quality cleaning but also professionalism, reliability, and consistency.

4. Meeting the Insurance Requirement: Historically, high-quality home cleaners struggled to enter the multifamily space due to insurance requirements. But with PINCH, you now have access to insurance coverage, eliminating a significant barrier to entry.

5. Streamlined Back Office Operations: PINCH has your back when it comes to issuing and managing invoices, handling accounting, and integrating seamlessly with vendor management systems. You can focus on what you do best – delivering top-notch cleaning services.

6. Automation: PINCH's automated scheduling software allows the order entry process to coordinate, connect and notify you in your calendar. Keeping schedules in a digital format helps to remove many common human errors that occur with scheduling/rescheduling jobs.

Your journey in this space promises not only financial success but also the chance to redefine the industry's standards. Embrace this moment, and seize the extraordinary potential that lies before you.

video.subtitle: Introduction to PINCH for Multifamily
video.description:
At PINCH, we understand the challenges that multifamily communities face when it comes to turnover cleaning. We've designed our platform to not only streamline the cleaning process but also to empower cleaning professionals like you to grow your business. Let's explore how PINCH benefits both multifamily communities and your business:

1. High Quality Cleaning Standards: PINCH ensures that your cleaning services meet the highest standards. Multifamily communities rely on us to connect them with cleaning professionals who are committed to delivering impeccable results.

These are the reasons we train home cleaners on the specifics of multifamily turnover cleaning. Your partnership with PINCH positions you as a trusted and quality-focused cleaner.

2. Vendor Management Integration: We've developed a platform that seamlessly integrates with multifamily communities' systems. Property managers require each vendor to apply to their vendor management system.

This process is time consuming and many cleaners are unable to get approved as they don't have the back office processes or the insurances that are needed to comply with the properties requirements. Property Managers appreciate the convenience of working with PINCH.

This integration makes it effortless for property managers to access your services, streamlining the entire process and reducing administrative burdens.

3. Trust and Reputation: By aligning your business with PINCH, you tap into our reputation for reliability and professionalism. Multifamily communities value trust when choosing cleaning partners.

Your affiliation with PINCH communicates trustworthiness and dedication to quality, which can lead to long-lasting relationships and repeat business.

4. Large Network: Our extensive network of cleaning professionals ensures that multifamily communities are never in a PINCH when they require reliable and high-quality cleaning resources.

Whether it's a last-minute turnover cleaning or ongoing maintenance, our network is equipped to meet their needs promptly and professionally, fostering trust and satisfaction among property managers and residents alike.

Our ability to add workers to your team at any time gives your company the impression that you are much larger than you really are.

5. Business Support and Scheduling: We provide essential tools and resources to help you manage and expand your cleaning business effectively. From insurance and invoice management to sales training and scheduling tools, we've got you covered.

Our platform is designed to make your operations smoother and more efficient. These benefits are passed onto the multifamily communities as they are able to rely on a larger platform that specializes in these areas.

Our scheduling features give you professional tools when managing clients and your jobs. We provide many ways to automatically communicate with your clients. PINCH provides a full suite of automated notifications that professionalize your presence in front of your clients.

6. No Upfront Costs: One of the key benefits of partnering with PINCH is that you can access these resources and support without any upfront costs. We're invested in your success, and our revenue is tied to yours.

We only prosper when you do, which means you can focus on growing your business without financial barriers. This also helps keep the cleaning prices down so that Property Managers are able to meet their monthly budgets.

PINCH acts as a bridge between multifamily communities seeking top-quality cleaning services and cleaning professionals looking to expand their businesses. Our platform ensures elevated cleaning standards, seamless integration, trust-building, and access to a thriving multifamily market.

video.subtitle: Success Stories
video.description:
Success Story 1: Maria's Cleaning Services
Maria, the owner of a small cleaning business, joined PINCH to explore opportunities in the multifamily turnover cleaning industry. With PINCH's support, she not only increased her revenue but also expanded her employee count.

Maria leveraged PINCH's network in the southeast to secure contracts with several multifamily communities. Within the first year, Maria's Cleaning Services grew from a three-person operation to a team of ten dedicated cleaners.

Her commitment to excellence and the seamless integration with multifamily communities through PINCH earned her a stellar reputation. Today, Maria's business continues to thrive, serving numerous properties and providing stable employment opportunities for her team.

Success Story 2: John's Sparkling Cleaners
John, a passionate cleaner with a small crew, partnered with PINCH to tap into the multifamily market.

With PINCH's training, technology, and support, John's Sparkling Cleaners quickly established itself as a go-to cleaning service for multifamily communities. Communities started asking for him by name where he started to close multifamily deals without having to sell.

His dedication to delivering exceptional turnover services, backed by PINCH's resources, set him apart. As the demand for his services soared, John expanded his team from 4 cleaners to a workforce of twelve.

His business revenues skyrocketed, and he became a trusted partner for several multifamily communities.

Thanks to PINCH's assistance, John not only achieved remarkable business growth but also provided stable employment opportunities for his team members, transforming his small cleaning business into a thriving enterprise. I'm nervous. I have never sold before

video.subtitle: Overcoming Sales Anxiety
video.description:
Introduction: Feeling nervous about selling your cleaning services in the multifamily industry is completely normal, especially if you've never considered yourself a "salesperson." However, it's essential to remember that selling is not as intimidating as it may seem.

In this section, we'll address your concerns and provide you with insights to help you overcome any anxiety related to selling.

Understanding the Numbers Game: First and foremost, it's crucial to recognize that sales is a numbers game. You may not win every potential client, and that's perfectly okay. Just like how you currently sell your services when you visit someone's home, selling to multifamily communities follows the same principle.

Some prospects will be interested, and some may not be, and that's a normal part of the process. Most sales entities look at their engagement and sales ratio based on “out of 100” method. In other words, if you stopped into 100 communities…how many would give you the time of day.

How many would spend a few minutes with you to learn what you have to offer. If you find 10, then you are doing great! From there, how many of the 10 can you close? 1? 3? 5? Over time, you will learn your close ratio. The more business you close, the easier it will be to get the next client.

Many of these Property Managers communicate with other communities and they share valuable vendor experiences.

Leveraging Your Existing Skills: Think about your current cleaning business. When you interact with homeowners or clients, you're essentially showcasing your services and persuading them to choose you. That's a form of sales! You already possess the skills to communicate the value of your services effectively.

Keep in mind that most of the communities you walk into have a cleaner today. You need to convey why or how you are different. The way to sell yourself is to highlight that you have a smaller, more dedicated team.

Most of your competitors are hourly workers that work for a larger commercial business. They won't value the partnership and relationship you will have with the community.

Don't Overthink It: Selling to multifamily communities should not be overthought. It's about presenting what you offer in a clear and straightforward manner. Remember, property managers are looking for reliable and quality cleaning services, just like your current clients. Approach the conversation with confidence, knowing that you have the expertise to meet their needs.

Practice Makes Perfect: If you're still feeling nervous, practice is your best ally. Start by rehearsing your pitch or presentation. Role-play with a friend or colleague to gain confidence in your sales approach. The more you practice, the more comfortable and natural your sales conversations will become.

Conclusion:
Selling may feel nerve-wracking initially, but remember that it's a skill you can develop over time. Embrace the fact that it's a numbers game, leverage your existing skills, and don't overthink it.

With practice and persistence, you'll find yourself becoming a more confident and successful salesperson in the multifamily cleaning industry.

video.subtitle: Building Rapport - Building Rapport with Community Managers as a Salesperson
video.description:
Introduction: As a salesperson for your cleaning business targeting the multifamily industry, establishing rapport with community managers is crucial to winning contracts.

In this lesson, we'll explore effective strategies to build relationships with these key decision-makers, earn their trust, and ultimately secure cleaning contracts for your company.

1. Research and Preparation: Before reaching out to community managers, conduct thorough research on their properties, including their specific cleaning needs and pain points.  Prepare a tailored pitch that demonstrates your understanding of their challenges and how your cleaning services can address them.

2. Professionalism and Presentation: Approach every interaction with professionalism and confidence. Dress appropriately and maintain a polished appearance. If appropriate, prepare professional presentations that showcase your cleaning services, emphasizing their benefits and value to their properties.

3. Active Listening: During meetings and discussions, practice active listening. Allow community managers to express their concerns and requirements. Ask probing questions to gain deeper insights into their cleaning needs and show genuine interest in their properties.

Don't spend too much time telling them about your business. They understand that you are a cleaning company. Sell the relationship and your personality rather than what services you offer.

4. Highlight Your Expertise: Position yourself as an expert in multifamily cleaning. Share success stories and case studies that highlight your experience in the industry. Emphasize your knowledge of turnover cleaning, maintenance, and other specialized services relevant to multifamily properties.

5. Tailor Your Solutions: Customize your cleaning proposals to align with the specific needs of each property. Tailored solutions demonstrate your commitment to meeting their requirements. Show flexibility in adapting your services to different property types and sizes.

6. Address Concerns Proactively: Anticipate common objections or concerns community managers might have, and prepare responses in advance. Be proactive in addressing these concerns during your discussions, offering solutions and reassurances.

7. Build Trust and Credibility: Share testimonials, references, or industry certifications to build trust and credibility. Highlight your track record of delivering exceptional cleaning services.
Encourage community managers to visit your completed projects to see the
quality of your work firsthand.

video.subtitle: Needs Assessment
video.description:
Introduction: Understanding the unique needs of community managers in the multifamily industry is essential for sales success. In this lesson, we'll explore the art of conducting a thorough needs assessment to uncover the specific challenges and requirements of community managers and how to position your cleaning services accordingly.

Lesson

Content: 1. Preparation: Before meeting with community managers, research their properties and the
multifamily industry as a whole.

2. Active Listening: During initial meetings or discussions, prioritize active listening. Allow community managers to express their concerns and objectives.
Ask open-ended questions to encourage them to share their needs, expectations, and pain points.

3. Empathy and Understanding: Show genuine empathy towards the challenges community managers face in maintaining their properties. Demonstrate your understanding of their responsibilities and the importance of maintaining a clean and appealing environment for residents before they move in.

4. Identify Pain Points: Probe for specific pain points or recurring issues they encounter with their current cleaning team. These could include inconsistent cleaning quality, scheduling difficulties, or budget constraints. Encourage them to share their experiences with past cleaning providers, both
positive and negative.

5. Tailor Your Solutions: Based on the information gathered, tailor your cleaning solutions to address their unique needs. Highlight how your services can alleviate their pain points. Present a customized plan that demonstrates your commitment to resolving their challenges effectively.

6. Offer Value and Benefits: Clearly articulate the value your cleaning services bring to their properties. Explain how your expertise can enhance tenant satisfaction, reduce turnover times, and maintain property value. Emphasize the benefits of partnering with your company.

One example would be to focus on how frequently the current cleaning company has to return to “re-clean” something that was missed. Not only does this delay the unit from being ready, but it also sucks up valuable time of the community manager and/or service manager.

7. Provide Solutions,

Not Just Services: Position your cleaning services as solutions to their problems. Show how your team can take the burden of turnover cleaning off their shoulders. Offer additional support, such as maintenance or scheduling assistance, to make their job easier. The PINCH tools can help you greatly with this.

8. Build Trust and Confidence: Share success stories of similar properties you've worked with to illustrate your track record. Share the name PINCH and how our platform was designed to ensure that no community would ever get into a “PINCH” again. We have a huge network of cleaners that can help assist your cleaning company at any time.

9. Collaborative Approach: Promote a collaborative partnership where you work together to achieve common goals. Encourage ongoing communication and feedback to ensure your services continuously meet their evolving needs.

Most community managers do not have a great line of communication with their cleaners. They are generally speaking to a manager or someone at corporate. This is an opportunity for you!

Conclusion:
Conducting effective needs assessments in multifamily sales is the foundation for building successful partnerships with community managers.

By actively listening, empathizing, and tailoring your solutions to address their pain points, you position yourself as a valuable partner dedicated to helping them achieve property excellence.

Remember, it's not just about offering cleaning services; it's about providing solutions that make their job easier and their properties more attractive to residents.

video.subtitle: Value Proposition
video.description:
Introduction: In the competitive world of multifamily sales, a strong value proposition is your key to success. In this lesson, we'll explore how to communicate the value your cleaning company and PINCH bring to property managers and why this partnership is a winning solution.

1. Your Cleaning Company's Value Proposition:

Quality Cleaning Services: Emphasize your commitment to delivering top-notch turnover cleaning services. Highlight your experienced team, attention to detail, and dedication to maintaining pristine properties.

Reliability: Stress the importance of consistency in cleaning schedules. Assure property managers that they can rely on your team to meet their cleaning needs promptly and consistently.

Time and Cost Savings: Showcase how your services save property managers time and money. Explain how efficient cleaning processes can reduce turnover times and lower overall maintenance costs.

Tenant Satisfaction: Emphasize the impact of clean and well-maintained properties on tenant satisfaction. Happy tenants are more likely to renew leases and recommend the property to others.

Tenant Satisfaction: Emphasize the impact of clean and well-maintained properties on tenant satisfaction. Happy tenants are more likely to renew leases and recommend the property to others.

2. PINCH's Value Proposition:

Scheduling Tools: With a focus on technology, PINCH's scheduling tools bring organization to a normally chaotic situation. These tools enable the Maintenance or Service Manager to know what has been booked, in progress, and completed. No more pointing fingers and getting frustrated at missed scheduling issues.

Insurance and Vendor Management: Highlight PINCH's provision of insurance coverage and seamless integration with vendor management systems. Explain how this eliminates the hassle for property managers, saving them time and reducing risks.

This is also one of the main reasons that you (a smaller cleaning company) can afford to work in a multifamily.

Streamlined
Invoicing and Back Office Support: Describe how PINCH simplifies the invoicing process, ensuring property managers receive accurate and timely invoices. Stress the convenience of having a reliable partner for back-office tasks.

Sales Training and Support: Emphasize PINCH's sales training resources. Mention that you have received certifications through PINCH for multifamily turnovers. You are equipped to complete turnover jobs in a timely fashion and at the highest quality levels.

Nationwide Network: Highlight the vast network of cleaners and resources available through PINCH. Explain that this network ensures that you always have access to reliable cleaning resources to fill in no matter how many jobs are required by the community.

Conclusion:
Crafting a compelling value proposition for multifamily sales involves communicating the unique strengths of your cleaning company and the added benefits of the partnership with PINCH.

By focusing on quality, reliability, cost savings, tenant satisfaction, and the comprehensive support provided by PINCH, you position yourself as the ideal solution for property managers looking to enhance their property management operations and tenant experiences.

video.subtitle: Handling Objections
video.description:
Introduction: In the world of multifamily sales, objections are a common roadblock on the path to success. This lesson will guide you through the art of handling objections with confidence, focusing on the most common objections small cleaning companies may encounter when selling to property managers.

1. Common Objections in Multifamily Sales:

Budget Constraints: Property managers may express concerns about budget limitations and may resist adding cleaning services at a higher price than they are paying today.

Current Cleaning Provider: Some managers may already have a cleaning service in place, and they might be hesitant to switch.

Lack of Time: Property managers often have packed schedules and may perceive onboarding a new cleaning service as time-consuming.

Quality Assurance: Managers may doubt your ability to deliver consistent, high-quality cleaning services.

Uncertainty About Multifamily: Small cleaning companies may face objections related to their limited experience and team size for managing the volume at multifamily communities.

2. Strategies for Handling Objections:

Active Listening: Begin by actively listening to the objection. Allow the property manager to express their concerns fully.

Empathize and Understand: Show empathy and understanding for their perspective. Acknowledge their concerns and validate their feelings.

Provide Solutions: Offer specific solutions to address their objections. For example, if budget constraints are the issue, focus on the cost of poor quality. If they are willing to talk to you today, they are likely experiencing poor quality, which takes away from their day to handle these situations. How valuable is their time?

Sales Training and Support: Emphasize PINCH's sales training resources. Mention that you have received certifications through PINCH for multifamily turnovers. You are equipped to complete turnover jobs in a timely fashion and at the highest quality levels.

Highlight Benefits: Emphasize the benefits of your cleaning services, improved tenant satisfaction, lack of callbacks, and efficient turnover times.

Address Experience Concerns: If experience in multifamily is questioned, share success stories or PINCH's training resources that demonstrate your commitment to excellence in this sector.

3. Role-Playing and Practice:

Engage in role-playing exercises to simulate objection scenarios. Practice your responses until you feel confident and natural in your
approach.

4. Handling Rejection:

Understand that not every objection can be overcome. Sometimes, property managers have legitimate reasons for their objections, and it's crucial to respect their decisions gracefully.

5. Continuous Learning:

Stay open to learning and adapting your sales approach. Seek feedback from peers or mentors to refine your objection-handling skills continually.

Conclusion:
Effectively handling objections is a valuable skill in multifamily sales. By actively listening, empathizing, and providing tailored solutions that highlight the benefits of your cleaning services, you can address common objections and build stronger relationships with property managers.

Remember that objections are opportunities for clarification and education, ultimately bringing you closer to successful multifamily sales.

video.subtitle: Closing Techniques
video.description:
Introduction: Closing the deal is the culmination of your efforts in multifamily sales. This lesson will explore essential closing techniques to help you secure commitments from property managers and turn prospects into loyal clients.

1. The Importance of Effective Closes: Closing is the final step in the sales process and is crucial for achieving your sales goals and growing your business.

2. Building Rapport: Before attempting to close, ensure you've built a strong rapport with the property manager. Trust and rapport significantly influence their decision to commit. If you have a joking relationship with the prospect, then use that to your advantage.

3. Trial Close: Use trial closes throughout the conversation to gauge the prospect's readiness. For example, ask questions like, "If we can meet your budget, would you be ready to proceed?"

4. Summarize Benefits: Recap the key benefits of your cleaning services and your
partnership with PINCH, emphasizing how they address the property manager's pain points and objectives.

5. Assumptive Close: Assume the sale by phrasing your questions in a way that implies their commitment. For example, "When would you like us to set up your first cleaning?"

6. Alternative Close: Present the property manager with a choice between two favorable options, both leading to a commitment. For instance, "Would you prefer we start next week, or would the following week work better for you?"

7. The Puppy Dog Close: Offer a trial period or a limited-time promotion to allow the property manager to experience your services before making a long-term commitment. Giving the community one free turnover cleaning is a great way to get your foot in the door and to prove your value.

8. The Takeaway Close: If the prospect seems hesitant, briefly mention a potential drawback or limitation of not proceeding with your services. This can encourage them to reconsider.

Example: "I completely understand your concerns, and it's important to make a decision you're comfortable with."I'll go ahead and prepare the necessary documentation and contracts. If you decide to move forward, we'll be ready to get started right away.

If you have any questions or need further information during your decision-making process, please don't hesitate to reach out to me. Your satisfaction is our top priority."

9. The Urgency Close: Create a sense of urgency by mentioning limited availability or special offers that expire soon. Urgency can nudge the prospect toward a quicker decision.

10. Silence and Patience: After presenting your offer, resist the urge to fill the silence. Allow the property manager time to think and respond. The person who speaks first in silence is usually the loser. Silence creates an un-comfortableness in most people.

This is when prospects tend to tell you what they are really thinking...simply because they don't like the silence any more and they don't know what else to say.

11. Handling Objections: Be prepared to address any final objections or concerns that may arise before closing. Use objection-handling techniques to reassure the prospect.

12. Ask for the Close: Clearly and confidently ask for the commitment. Avoid vague or ambiguous language. For example, "Can we move forward with this agreement today?"

13. Trial Close (Again): Before concluding the conversation, use another trial close to gauge their response. If they show signs of commitment, proceed to finalize the deal.

14. Express Gratitude: Regardless of the outcome, express gratitude for their time and consideration. Maintain professionalism and leave the door open for future discussions.

15. Follow-Up: If the property manager needs more time to decide, schedule a follow-up call or meeting to revisit the proposal and address any remaining questions or concerns. Avoid saying "I will follow up with you...(on a date)." Instead, say "how does next Tuesday at 2:00 work for you?

We can meet very briefly and decide if this is something you want to move forward with or not." Taking an approach like this will get you to a "yes" or "no"answer. Using the words "follow up" is a very non-urgent communication and keeps most prospects hanging on rather than saying yes or no.

Conclusion:
Effective closing techniques are essential for converting prospects into satisfied clients in multifamily sales. By using a combination of approaches, understanding your prospect's needs, and maintaining professionalism, you can increase your success rate and build lasting relationships with property managers.

Remember that each prospect is unique, so adapt your closing approach to suit their specific circumstances and preferences.

questions.question: What is one primary pain point property managers often face when it comes to cleaning services in multifamily communities?
options: Poor quality from the cleaning companies
Too many cleaning companies to choose from

questions.question: What is the average revenue per building that a cleaner can expect in multifamily cleaning?
What does the "Turn Clean" refer to in the multifamily cleaning industry?
options: A clean performed before moving in
A cleaning performed after the unit has been cleaned once, but extra dirt/dust has fallen

questions.question: What should a cleaner focus on during a needs assessment with a community manager?
options: The manager's unique needs and challenges

questions.question: What is the main message to convey when handling objections in the sales process?
options: Try to convince the client to change their mind

questions.question: What is a value proposition in the context of selling cleaning services to property managers?
options: A statement of unique benefits offered

questions.question: When should a salesperson consider using the Assumptive Close technique?
options: When they want to pressure the client
When they are unsure about the sale
When they are confident the client is ready to buy
When they want to avoid objections

questions.question: What is a common objection a property manager might raise when approached by a cleaning company?
options: "We're already satisfied with our current cleaners."

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Closing Techniques in the sales process?
options: To gain commitment and close the sale

questions.question: Why is it important for a cleaner to understand the unique needs of a multifamily community during the sales process?
options: To prepare a generic sales pitch
To tailor the cleaning solution to the community's requirements
